boardFeetPage.intro: Board feet is the standard unit of measurement for lumber in North America. Whether you're buying hardwood for furniture, softwood for framing, or lumber for a DIY project, understanding how to calculate board feet ensures you buy the right amount and don't overpay.
boardFeetPage.whatIsP1: A board foot is a unit of volume for lumber, equal to 1 inch thick × 12 inches wide × 12 inches long (144 cubic inches). It's the standard way lumberyards and suppliers measure and price wood, especially hardwoods.
boardFeetPage.whatIsP2: Unlike linear feet (which only measures length) or square feet (which measures area), board feet accounts for all three dimensions — thickness, width, and length — giving you the true volume of wood.
boardFeetPage.formulaP1: The formula for calculating board feet is straightforward:
boardFeetPage.formulaBox:
Board Feet = (Thickness × Width × Length) ÷ 12
(Thickness and width in inches, length in feet)
boardFeetPage.example1Desc: A standard 2×4 that is 8 feet long: (2 × 4 × 8) ÷ 12 = 5.33 board feet
boardFeetPage.example2Desc: A 1×6 board 10 feet long: (1 × 6 × 10) ÷ 12 = 5.0 board feet
boardFeetPage.example3Desc: A 2×10 plank 12 feet long: (2 × 10 × 12) ÷ 12 = 20.0 board feet
boardFeetPage.vsP1: Linear feet measures only length, regardless of width or thickness. It's commonly used for softwood lumber (2x4s, 2x6s) sold at home centers. Board feet measures volume and is standard for hardwood lumber.
boardFeetPage.vsP2: When comparing prices: board foot pricing accounts for the actual wood volume, making it more accurate for cost comparison across different sizes. Linear foot pricing is simpler but doesn't account for material volume differences.
boardFeetPage.pricingP1: Hardwood lumber is typically priced per board foot. Prices vary by species, grade, and thickness. Common hardwoods range from $3-15 per board foot, while premium species can cost $20-50+ per board foot.
boardFeetPage.pricingP2: Softwood lumber at home centers is usually priced per linear foot. For example, a 2×4 might cost $0.50-1.50 per linear foot depending on wood type and treatment.
boardFeetPage.chartP1: Here's a quick reference for common lumber sizes and their board feet per foot of length. Use this to estimate without calculating:
boardFeetPage.tip1Desc: A 2×4 is actually 1.5 inches by 3.5 inches. For precise board foot calculations, use the actual dimensions rather than nominal sizes.
boardFeetPage.tip2Desc: Always measure your project and calculate board feet before going to the lumber yard. Bring your measurements to compare pricing across different sizes.
boardFeetPage.tip3Desc: Add 10-15% extra for waste, especially when working with hardwood where grain direction and defects can reduce usable material.
boardFeetPage.summaryP1: Board feet is essential knowledge for anyone working with lumber. The formula (T × W × L) ÷ 12 is simple once you understand it, and mastering it will save you money and prevent material shortages.
boardFeetPage.summaryP2: Use our free lumber calculator at the top of this guide for instant board feet calculations — just enter your dimensions and get results in seconds.
boardFeetPage.ctaDesc: Use our free lumber calculator for instant results. Enter your dimensions and get board feet, linear feet, and cost estimates.

lumberCalcPage.ctaBannerDesc: Calculate board feet, linear feet, and cost in seconds with our free online tool.
lumberCalcPage.whatIsP1: Board feet (BF) is the standard unit of volume measurement for lumber in North America. One board foot equals 144 cubic inches — typically defined as a board that is 1 inch thick, 12 inches wide, and 1 foot (12 inches) long. Understanding board feet is essential for buying lumber, estimating project costs, and communicating with suppliers.
lumberCalcPage.whatIsP2: When you buy hardwood lumber, it's almost always priced by the board foot. Softwood lumber at home centers is usually priced by the linear foot, but larger projects still require board foot calculations for accurate material estimation.
lumberCalcPage.whatIsTip: Think of board feet as the volume of wood. One board foot is roughly the size of a 1×12 board that's 12 inches long. This volume-based measurement helps you compare prices across different lumber sizes fairly.
lumberCalcPage.howToP1: The formula for calculating board feet is:
lumberCalcPage.formulaDesc: Where T = Thickness (inches), W = Width (inches), L = Length (feet)
lumberCalcPage.howToP2: If you have measurements in different units, convert everything to inches for thickness and width, and feet for length before applying the formula.
lumberCalcPage.stepsIntro: Follow these simple steps to calculate board feet for any lumber:
lumberCalcPage.step1Desc: Measure the thickness and width of the lumber in inches. Use a tape measure for accuracy — remember that nominal sizes differ from actual sizes.
lumberCalcPage.step2Desc: Measure the length of the board in feet. If your measurement is in inches, divide by 12 to convert to feet.
lumberCalcPage.step3Desc: Multiply thickness × width × length, then divide by 12. The result is board feet for that piece of lumber.
lumberCalcPage.step4Desc: If you need multiple pieces of the same size, multiply the board feet per piece by the total quantity needed.
lumberCalcPage.sizesP1: Here's a quick reference for common nominal lumber sizes, their actual dimensions, and the board feet per foot of length:
lumberCalcPage.tip1Desc: A 2×4 is actually 1.5" × 3.5". Using nominal sizes in calculations will overestimate your board feet by about 30%. Always measure or look up the actual dimensions.
lumberCalcPage.tip2Desc: Add 10-15% to your total for waste. Knots, warping, grain defects, and cutting errors all reduce usable material. For premium projects, consider 20% waste.
lumberCalcPage.tip3Desc: Home centers often price softwood by linear foot, while lumber yards price hardwood by board foot. Know which unit you're being quoted before comparing prices.
lumberCalcPage.tip4Desc: Our free lumber calculator handles all the math for you — just enter your dimensions and get instant board feet, linear feet, and cost estimates.
lumberCalcPage.summaryP1: Calculating board feet is a fundamental skill for any woodworking or construction project. The formula is simple — thickness × width × length ÷ 12 — but accurate measurements are everything.
lumberCalcPage.summaryP2: Remember: use actual dimensions (not nominal), include waste factor, and always double-check your math before buying materials. For quick calculations, bookmark our lumber calculator tool.
lumberCalcPage.ctaDesc: Use our free online lumber calculator to get instant board feet, linear feet, and cost estimates for your next project.

lumberSizesPage.intro: Understanding lumber sizes is one of the most confusing aspects of woodworking and construction for beginners. A "2×4" isn't actually 2 inches by 4 inches, and the rules differ between softwood and hardwood. This guide explains everything you need to know about lumber dimensions, types, and grades.
lumberSizesPage.nominalP1: Nominal size is the name used to identify lumber, like "2×4" or "1×6." These names date back to when lumber was rough-sawn at those exact dimensions. Today, lumber is planed and dried before sale, which reduces its dimensions.
lumberSizesPage.nominalP2: The actual size is the true dimension after planing. For softwood lumber: a nominal 2×4 is actually 1½ inches by 3½ inches. A nominal 1×6 is actually ¾ inch by 5½ inches. The difference is consistent across standard sizes.
lumberSizesPage.dimensionsIntro: Here are the most common softwood lumber sizes with their nominal names, actual dimensions, and typical uses:
lumberSizesPage.2x10: Ridge boards, stair stringers, large beams
lumberSizesPage.2x12: Ridge boards, stair stringers, heavy beams
lumberSizesPage.softwoodP1: Softwoods (pine, fir, spruce, cedar) are sized using nominal dimensions. A 2×4 softwood board is never actually 2" × 4". Softwood is typically sold at home centers and lumber yards priced by the linear foot.
lumberSizesPage.hardwoodP1: Hardwoods (oak, maple, walnut, cherry) are typically sold in rough thicknesses (4/4, 6/4, 8/4 — pronounced "four-quarter," etc.) and priced by the board foot. A 4/4 board is 1 inch thick, 6/4 is 1.5 inches thick.
lumberSizesPage.treatedP1: Pressure treated lumber has been chemically treated to resist rot, decay, and insect damage. It's commonly used for outdoor projects like decks, fences, and retaining walls. Pressure treated lumber is typically available in the same nominal sizes as standard softwood.
lumberSizesPage.treatedP2: Note that pressure treated lumber is often slightly larger than its untreated equivalent because the treatment process can cause swelling. It also takes several months to fully dry out after purchase.
lumberSizesPage.grade1Desc: The highest grade, with minimal knots and defects. Used for critical structural applications where strength and appearance matter.
lumberSizesPage.grade2Desc: High-quality lumber with small, sound knots allowed. Suitable for most construction and woodworking projects.
lumberSizesPage.chooseTitle: How to Choose the Right Lumber
lumberSizesPage.chooseP1: Choosing the right lumber for your project depends on several factors: structural requirements, appearance expectations, budget, and the environment where the lumber will be used.
lumberSizesPage.chooseP2: For structural framing, stick with No. 2 or better grade softwood in standard dimensional sizes (2×4, 2×6, etc.). For visible woodworking projects, choose hardwood or premium-grade softwood. For outdoor projects, use pressure treated lumber or naturally rot-resistant species like cedar or redwood.
lumberSizesPage.summaryP1: Understanding lumber sizes and types is essential for any construction or woodworking project. Remember: nominal size is just a name — always use actual dimensions for measurements and calculations.
lumberSizesPage.summaryP2: Use our lumber calculator and buying guides to save time and money on your next project. When in doubt, consult with your local lumber yard — they can help you select the right grade and species.
lumberSizesPage.ctaDesc: Use our free lumber calculator to estimate how much material you need.
lumberSizesPage.relatedGuide1Desc: Step-by-step guide with formulas and examples for calculating board feet.
lumberSizesPage.relatedGuide2Desc: Master board feet calculations with formula, examples, and pricing tips.
lumberSizesPage.grade4Desc: Economy grade with larger knots and more defects. Suitable for temporary structures, bracing, and applications where appearance doesn't matter.
lumberSizesPage.grade3Desc: The most common grade for framing. Allows larger knots and minor defects. Good value for structural applications where appearance isn't critical.
lumberSizesPage.nominalProTip: Pro Tip: Always measure before cutting. A "2x4" is never actually 2 inches by 4 inches. Planning with actual dimensions in mind saves time, money, and wasted material.
lumberSizesPage.softwoodSafetyNote: Safety Note: Always wear gloves and a dust mask when cutting pressure treated lumber. Never burn treated wood — it releases toxic chemicals.
lumberSizesPage.chooseStep1Desc: Structural framing, finish carpentry, outdoor projects, and furniture each demand different lumber grades and types.
lumberSizesPage.chooseStep2Desc: Codes dictate minimum grade, treatment, and span ratings for structural applications.
lumberSizesPage.chooseStep3Desc: Indoor, outdoor above ground, and ground contact each require different treatment levels.
lumberSizesPage.chooseStep4Desc: Order 10-15% extra to account for cuts, knots, warped boards, and future repairs.

deckBuildPage.intro: Building a deck is one of the most rewarding DIY projects you can undertake. A well-built deck adds living space, increases property value, and provides a perfect outdoor gathering spot. This guide walks you through every step from planning to finishing.
deckBuildPage.ctaTitle: Plan Your Deck with Our Calculator
deckBuildPage.ctaDesc: Use our free deck calculator to estimate materials and costs before you start building.
deckBuildPage.planningP1: Before you break ground, check your local building codes and permit requirements. Most municipalities require permits for decks over a certain height or size. Draw a detailed plan including dimensions, post locations, and material specifications.
deckBuildPage.planningP2: Consider your deck's orientation — south-facing decks get more sun, while north-facing stays cooler. Account for existing trees, drainage patterns, and access points for material delivery.
deckBuildPage.materialsP1: The three most common decking materials are pressure-treated wood, cedar/redwood, and composite decking. Pressure-treated lumber is most affordable ($2-4 per sq ft), cedar offers natural beauty ($4-7 per sq ft), and composite requires minimal maintenance ($6-12 per sq ft).
deckBuildPage.materialsP2: For the frame, use pressure-treated lumber or tropical hardwood. Choose galvanized or stainless steel hardware to prevent corrosion. Your decking board choice affects both appearance and long-term maintenance.
deckBuildPage.foundationP1: Deck foundations typically use concrete footings with post anchors. The depth depends on your frost line — usually 12-48 inches deep. Space posts according to your deck size and local codes, typically 6-8 feet apart.
deckBuildPage.foundationP2: Frame with double beams at post tops, then install joists perpendicular to the beams at 16 or 24 inches on center. Use joist hangers for every connection and install blocking between joists for stability.
deckBuildPage.deckingP1: Start decking installation from the house side, working outward. Leave a 1/8-inch gap between boards for drainage and expansion. Stagger end joints across different joists for a stronger, more attractive surface. Use two screws or hidden fasteners at every joist crossing.
deckBuildPage.railingsP1: Railings are required for decks over 30 inches high. Most codes require rails at least 36 inches tall with balusters spaced no more than 4 inches apart. For stairs, calculate rise and run carefully — typical step dimensions are a 7-inch rise with an 11-inch tread.
deckBuildPage.finishingP1: If using wood decking, apply a water-repellent sealer or stain within a few weeks of installation. Clean the deck first and apply on a dry day above 50°F. Reapply every 1-3 years depending on climate and sun exposure.
deckBuildPage.tip1Desc: Always call your local utility locating service before digging post holes. They'll mark underground gas, electric, and water lines for free.
deckBuildPage.tip2Desc: Order 10-15% extra decking material to account for cuts, waste, and future repairs. Return unopened bundles if unused.
deckBuildPage.tip3Desc: Hidden deck fasteners create a cleaner appearance and prevent screw holes that can collect water and lead to rot.
deckBuildPage.summaryP1: Building a deck is a significant project, but careful planning and the right materials ensure lasting results. Use our deck calculator to estimate materials and costs, then follow this guide step by step.

deckMaterialsPage.intro: Accurately calculating deck materials is essential for budgeting and avoiding costly shortages or waste. Whether you're building a small platform deck or a large multi-level structure, knowing exactly what you need saves time and money.
deckMaterialsPage.ctaDesc: Use our free deck calculator to get accurate material quantities for your deck project.
deckMaterialsPage.measuringP1: Start by measuring the total deck area in square feet. Multiply length by width for rectangular decks. For irregular shapes, divide the area into rectangles and triangles, calculate each, and add them together.
deckMaterialsPage.measuringP2: Record all measurements in feet and inches. Draw a detailed sketch showing dimensions, post locations, joist direction, and any obstructions like trees or existing structures.
deckMaterialsPage.deckingP1: To calculate decking boards, divide the total deck width by the width of your chosen board (accounting for gaps). Multiply by the deck length for linear feet needed. Add 10% for waste and cuts.
deckMaterialsPage.deckingP2: Board dimensions matter — standard 5/4×6 decking actually measures 5.5 inches wide, while 2×6 measures 5.5 inches. Always use actual dimensions for calculations.
deckMaterialsPage.joistsP1: Joist spacing depends on decking material: 16 inches on center for wood, 12 inches for composite. Divide the deck dimension perpendicular to joists by the spacing to get joist count.
deckMaterialsPage.joistsP2: Beams must be sized according to span and load. A single 2×8 beam can span about 6 feet, while a double 2×10 can span up to 10 feet. Consult span tables or use our calculator for accurate sizing.
deckMaterialsPage.fastenersP1: Plan for 2-3 pounds of deck screws per 100 square feet of decking. Joist hangers require specific nails — typically 1-1/2 inch hot-dipped galvanized. Hidden fastener systems come with manufacturer-specified quantities per square foot.
deckMaterialsPage.wasteP1: Always add a waste factor of 10-15% to all material calculations. This accounts for miscuts, damaged boards, and future repairs. For diagonal decking patterns, increase waste to 20%.
deckMaterialsPage.tip1Desc: Lumber sizes vary by region. Verify actual dimensions at your local supplier before finalizing calculations.
deckMaterialsPage.tip2Desc: Many suppliers offer bundle pricing for full units. Calculate whether buying full bundles is more economical than individual boards.
deckMaterialsPage.summaryP1: Accurate material calculation prevents delays and budget overruns. Use our deck calculator for precise measurements, then verify with local suppliers before purchasing.

deckCostPage.intro: Understanding deck costs helps you budget effectively and make informed decisions about materials and design. A deck is a significant investment, and knowing where your money goes ensures you get the best value.
deckCostPage.ctaDesc: Use our free deck calculator with cost estimation features to budget your project.
deckCostPage.materialsP1: Materials account for 40-60% of total deck cost. A basic pressure-treated wood deck costs $15-25 per sq ft, while composite decking runs $25-40 per sq ft. Include framing lumber, decking, fasteners, railings, and concrete for footings.
deckCostPage.materialsP2: Premium materials like Ipe or tropical hardwood can cost $30-50 per sq ft. Factor in delivery fees, which range from $50-200 depending on distance and quantity.
deckCostPage.laborP1: Professional installation adds $8-15 per sq ft for basic decks and $15-25 per sq ft for complex designs with stairs, multiple levels, or custom features. DIY installation saves labor costs but requires tools, skills, and time.
deckCostPage.permitsP1: Building permits typically cost $100-500 depending on your municipality and deck size. Some areas require engineered plans for complex decks, adding $300-800. Factor in HOA approval fees if applicable.
deckCostPage.costBySizeP1: A 10×10 deck (100 sq ft) costs $1,500-4,000 for materials alone. A 12×16 deck (192 sq ft) ranges $3,000-7,500. Large 20×20 decks (400 sq ft) can cost $6,000-16,000+. Costs scale with complexity, not just size.
deckCostPage.savingP1: Save money by keeping the design simple — rectangular decks cost less than angled or multi-level designs. Use pressure-treated lumber instead of composite for the frame. Install standard 16-inch joist spacing instead of 12-inch.
deckCostPage.savingP2: Buy materials during off-season (fall/winter) for potential discounts. Consider doing the demolition and site preparation yourself to save on labor costs.
deckCostPage.summaryP1: Deck costs vary widely based on materials, size, and labor. Use our deck calculator with cost estimation to get a personalized budget, and always get multiple contractor quotes for installation.

fenceCalcPage.intro: Calculating fence materials accurately ensures you buy the right quantities without waste or shortages. Whether installing a privacy fence, picket fence, or garden enclosure, knowing your material needs is the first step to a successful project.
fenceCalcPage.ctaDesc: Use our free fence calculator to estimate posts, rails, pickets, and concrete.
fenceCalcPage.measuringP1: Start by measuring the total fence length in feet. Mark property lines clearly and confirm boundaries with a survey if needed. Account for gates, corners, and changes in elevation.
fenceCalcPage.measuringP2: Draw a plan showing post locations, gate openings, and any obstacles like trees or rocks. Measure twice to ensure accuracy before ordering materials.
fenceCalcPage.postsP1: Divide the total fence length by the post spacing to get the number of posts. Standard spacing is 6-8 feet apart. Add one post for each corner, gate end, and end of fence run.
fenceCalcPage.postsP2: Post height equals fence height plus 2-3 feet buried depth. For a 6-foot fence, use 8-9 foot posts. Choose 4×4 posts for most residential fences, or 6×6 for heavy gates and tall fences.
fenceCalcPage.railsP1: Most fences use 2 or 3 horizontal rails per section. Multiply the number of sections by number of rails. Standard 2×4 lumber works for most residential fences. For long spans, consider 2×6 rails for extra strength.
fenceCalcPage.picketsP1: For picket fences, divide the total fence length by the picket spacing (center-to-center) to get picket count. Standard picket spacing ranges from 2-5 inches apart depending on the desired look and privacy level.
fenceCalcPage.concreteP1: Each post hole needs about 50-60 pounds of dry concrete mix (one bag). For sandy soil or extra stability, use 80 pounds per post. Calculate total bags by multiplying post count by bag quantity per post.
fenceCalcPage.wasteP1: Add 10% to all material quantities for waste, cuts, and mistakes. For pickets, add 15% since individual pickets are more likely to be damaged during installation.
fenceCalcPage.tip1Desc: Always verify property lines before installing fences. A survey costs less than relocating a fence built on your neighbor's property.
fenceCalcPage.tip2Desc: Use exterior-grade screws instead of nails for attaching rails and pickets. Screws provide stronger connections and are easier to remove if repairs are needed.
fenceCalcPage.summaryP1: Accurate material calculations prevent project delays and waste. Use our fence calculator for precise quantities, and always verify measurements before purchasing materials.

fenceTypesPage.intro: Choosing the right fence type involves balancing cost, maintenance, privacy, and aesthetics. Each fencing material has distinct advantages and drawbacks. This guide compares the most common options to help you make an informed decision.
fenceTypesPage.ctaDesc: Use our free fence calculator with cost estimation features to compare material costs.
fenceTypesPage.woodP1: Wood is the most traditional and popular fencing material. Pine and cedar are common choices — pine is affordable ($12-18 per linear foot installed) but requires regular staining or sealing. Cedar naturally resists rot and insects ($18-30 per linear foot).
fenceTypesPage.woodP2: Wood fences offer complete privacy when built with close pickets or boards. They can be painted any color but need maintenance every 2-3 years. With proper care, a wood fence lasts 10-15 years.
fenceTypesPage.vinylP1: Vinyl fencing costs more upfront ($20-35 per linear foot installed) but requires virtually no maintenance. It won't rot, warp, or need painting. Vinyl is available in various styles including privacy, picket, and ranch rail.
fenceTypesPage.vinylP2: High-quality vinyl fencing lasts 20-30 years. It's impact-resistant but can become brittle in extreme cold. Color is molded throughout the material, so scratches are less visible than on painted surfaces.
fenceTypesPage.chainLinkP1: Chain-link is the most economical option ($8-15 per linear foot installed). It provides security and boundary marking without blocking views. Galvanized chain-link resists rust, and vinyl-coated options add color and longer life.
fenceTypesPage.aluminumP1: Aluminum fencing offers an elegant look similar to wrought iron at a lower cost ($20-30 per linear foot installed). It's lightweight, rust-resistant, and requires minimal maintenance. Ideal for decorative boundaries, pool enclosures, and front yards.
fenceTypesPage.comparisonP1: For a typical 150-foot fence: chain-link costs $1,200-2,250, wood costs $1,800-4,500, vinyl costs $3,000-5,250, and aluminum costs $3,000-4,500. Factor in long-term maintenance costs — wood needs periodic staining while vinyl and aluminum do not.
fenceTypesPage.summaryP1: The best fence material depends on your priorities: budget, privacy, maintenance, and aesthetics. Use our fence calculator to estimate material quantities and costs for your chosen fence type.

fencePostPage.intro: Proper fence post spacing is critical for a sturdy, long-lasting fence. Posts that are too far apart cause sagging, while posts too close together waste materials and increase costs. This guide covers standard spacing recommendations and how to calculate exact post positions.
fencePostPage.ctaDesc: Use our free fence calculator to determine exact post spacing and quantities for your project.
fencePostPage.standardP1: Standard fence post spacing is 6 to 8 feet apart for most residential fences. Six-foot spacing provides maximum strength and is recommended for privacy fences that catch wind. Eight-foot spacing works well for low decorative fences and chain-link.
fencePostPage.standardP2: For corner posts and gate posts, use heavier 6×6 posts instead of standard 4×4. These bear more load and need extra stability. Gate openings of 4 feet or wider require reinforced posts with additional concrete.
fencePostPage.byFenceTypeP1: Chain-link fences typically use 10-foot post spacing with top rail support. Wood privacy fences use 6-8 foot spacing. Vinyl fences follow manufacturer specifications — usually 6-8 feet. Horse fences and farm fences may use 8-12 foot spacing for rail fences.
fencePostPage.byFenceTypeP2: Always check local building codes, which may specify maximum post spacing for your area. Windy locations may require closer spacing for all fence types.
fencePostPage.soilP1: Soil type affects post stability. In loose or sandy soil, reduce spacing to 6 feet and bury posts deeper (36 inches minimum). In clay soil, 8-foot spacing with 24-inch depth often suffices. Rocky soil may require specialized post hole digging equipment.
fencePostPage.concreteP1: Bury posts at least 1/3 of their total length. For a 6-foot fence, dig 30-inch deep holes (below frost line in cold climates). Use 50-60 pounds of dry concrete mix per post hole. Keep the top 2-3 inches of concrete above ground to prevent water pooling.
fencePostPage.concreteP2: Ensure posts are perfectly plumb and aligned before concrete sets. Brace posts in both directions and check alignment with a string line. Allow concrete to cure for 24-48 hours before attaching rails.
fencePostPage.calculatingP1: Divide total fence length by desired post spacing, then add 1 for the starting post. Add extra posts for each corner, gate, and end. For a 150-foot fence with 8-foot spacing: 150 ÷ 8 = 18.75, round up to 19, plus corner posts = approximately 22 posts total.
fencePostPage.summaryP1: Proper post spacing ensures a fence that stands straight and lasts for years. Use our fence calculator to determine exact requirements for your specific project.

drywallCalcPage.intro: Calculating drywall accurately prevents material shortages and reduces waste. Whether you're finishing a basement, renovating a room, or building new construction, knowing exactly how many sheets, how much joint compound, and how many screws you need saves time and money.
drywallCalcPage.ctaDesc: Use our free drywall calculator to estimate sheets, joint compound, tape, and screws for any room.
drywallCalcPage.measuringP1: Calculate the total square footage by measuring each wall's length and height, then multiplying. Add ceiling area separately. For rectangular rooms: (wall perimeter × height) + (ceiling length × width).
drywallCalcPage.measuringP2: Account for different wall heights and angled ceilings. Measure each wall independently rather than assuming all walls are identical. Record measurements in a sketch for reference.
drywallCalcPage.openingsP1: Subtract door and window areas from your total. Standard doors are approximately 20 square feet each. Standard windows are approximately 15 square feet each. For large picture windows or sliding glass doors, measure and subtract actual dimensions.
drywallCalcPage.sheetsP1: Divide the adjusted total square footage by the sheet size. A standard 4×8 sheet covers 32 square feet, while 4×12 sheets cover 48 square feet. Round up to the nearest whole sheet.
drywallCalcPage.sheetsP2: For ceilings, consider using 4×12 sheets for fewer joints and a smoother finish. For walls, 4×8 sheets are easier to handle. Plan sheet layout to minimize waste — measure each wall's height and choose sheets accordingly.
drywallCalcPage.compoundP1: Plan for 1 gallon of joint compound per 100 square feet of drywall, plus extra for corners and details. One roll of paper tape covers about 250 linear feet of joints. Mesh tape is self-adhesive but requires more compound for embedding.
drywallCalcPage.screwsP1: Use approximately 1 pound of drywall screws per 100 square feet of drywall. Screws should be spaced 12 inches apart on ceilings and 16 inches on walls. For 4×8 sheets: about 32 screws per sheet on walls, 48 on ceilings.
drywallCalcPage.wasteP1: Add 10-15% to sheet counts for waste, cuts, and mistakes. Add 20% for complex rooms with many corners, angled ceilings, or openings. It's better to have extra sheets than to run short mid-project.
drywallCalcPage.tip1Desc: Hang drywall sheets horizontally (parallel to the floor) for stronger walls with fewer vertical joints to tape and mud.
drywallCalcPage.tip2Desc: Leave a 1/4-inch gap between sheets and the floor to prevent moisture wicking. Leave 1/8-inch gaps between sheets for expansion.
drywallCalcPage.tip3Desc: Stagger end joints between rows so they don't line up. This creates a stronger, crack-resistant wall surface.
drywallCalcPage.summaryP1: Accurate drywall calculations help you budget and plan effectively. Use our drywall calculator for instant estimates, then add 10-15% for waste to ensure you have enough material.

drywallTypesPage.intro: Choosing the right drywall type and size is essential for a professional finish. Different applications require different thicknesses, sizes, and specialized board types. This guide helps you select the best drywall for your project.
drywallTypesPage.ctaDesc: Use our free drywall calculator to estimate quantities and costs for any room size.
drywallTypesPage.sizesP1: The most common drywall size is 4×8 feet (32 sq ft per sheet). Other common sizes include 4×10 (40 sq ft) and 4×12 (48 sq ft). Taller sheets reduce horizontal joints for a smoother finish on high walls.
drywallTypesPage.sizesP2: Use 4×8 sheets for standard 8-foot walls. For 9-foot or 10-foot ceilings, consider 4×10 or 4×12 sheets to minimize joints. Larger sheets are heavier and may require two people to install.
drywallTypesPage.thicknessP1: 1/4-inch drywall is used for curved surfaces and as a skim coat over existing walls. 3/8-inch is sometimes used in remodeling. 1/2-inch is the standard for most residential walls and ceilings. 5/8-inch (Type X) is fire-rated, required between garages and living spaces, and recommended for ceilings.
drywallTypesPage.thicknessP2: 5/8-inch drywall provides better soundproofing and is more resistant to sagging on ceilings. Many building codes require 5/8-inch for ceiling installations to prevent sag between joists.
drywallTypesPage.specialtyP1: Moisture-resistant (green board or purple board) is designed for bathrooms, basements, and kitchens. Mold-resistant drywall includes additives to prevent mold growth. Fire-rated (Type X) contains glass fibers for fire resistance. Soundproof drywall has dense cores for noise reduction.
drywallTypesPage.choosingP1: For most rooms, standard 1/2-inch drywall is sufficient. Use moisture-resistant drywall in bathrooms and basements. Use fire-rated 5/8-inch drywall in garages, near furnaces, and between living units. For home theaters or bedrooms, consider soundproof drywall for better acoustics.
drywallTypesPage.summaryP1: Selecting the correct drywall type and thickness ensures code compliance, durability, and a professional finish. Use our drywall calculator to determine quantities once you've chosen the right board type.

drywallCostPage.intro: Estimating drywall costs accurately helps you budget for your project and avoid surprises. Costs include materials, labor, finishing supplies, and potential disposal fees. This guide breaks down each cost component for a complete budget picture.
drywallCostPage.ctaDesc: Use our free drywall calculator with cost estimation for material and labor budgeting.
drywallCostPage.materialsP1: Standard 1/2-inch drywall sheets cost $12-18 per sheet. Moisture-resistant drywall runs $15-22 per sheet. Fire-rated 5/8-inch drywall costs $18-25 per sheet. Joint compound is $12-18 per bucket, tape $5-8 per roll, and screws $8-12 per box.
drywallCostPage.materialsP2: For a typical 500 sq ft room, expect $200-400 in drywall materials alone. Metal corner bead costs $2-5 per 8-foot length. Include delivery fees if not picking up materials yourself.
drywallCostPage.laborP1: Professional drywall installation costs $1.50-3.00 per sq ft for hanging only, or $3.00-5.00 per sq ft fully finished (taped, mudded, and sanded). For a 500 sq ft room, expect $750-2,500 for complete installation.
drywallCostPage.finishingP1: Drywall finishing — taping, mudding, and sanding — is the most labor-intensive part. Level 1 finish (basic tape) is cheapest but not suitable for living spaces. Level 5 finish (smooth with skim coat) costs most but provides the best surface for paint or wallpaper.
drywallCostPage.costBySizeP1: A small room (100 sq ft walls + ceiling) costs $150-400 for DIY or $300-800 professionally installed. A medium room (300 sq ft) runs $400-1,200 DIY or $900-2,400 professionally. A large basement (800+ sq ft) costs $1,000-3,200 DIY or $2,400-6,000+ professionally finished.
drywallCostPage.savingP1: Save money by hanging drywall yourself and hiring a pro only for finishing — the most skill-intensive part. Buy full pallets of drywall (40-50 sheets) for lower per-sheet pricing. Use standard 4×8 sheets instead of larger sizes that require extra help.
drywallCostPage.savingP2: Combine orders with neighbors or friends for bulk discounts. Consider renting a drywall lift for ceilings — it's safer and faster than scaffolding or wrestling sheets overhead.
drywallCostPage.summaryP1: Drywall costs vary significantly based on room size, material choices, and whether you DIY or hire professionals. Use our drywall calculator with cost estimation features to get a personalized budget for your project.
